paper_title: Revealing Algorithmic Deductive Circuits for Logical Reasoning

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Recent studies have shown that Large Language Models (LLMs) can achieve strong reasoning performance by incorporating functional symbolic representations that abstractly describe graph traversal algorithms and step-by-step reasoning in few-shot learning settings. However, it remains unclear how LLMs genuinely understand the abstract meaning of each reasoning step and the overall algorithm from only a limited number of demonstrations. This work aims to localize the attention heads responsible for individual reasoning steps and characterize the types of information transferred among them. We first align constituent reasoning steps with their corresponding token logits under a symbolic-aided Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting framework. Our analysis shows that token positions that steer the reasoning process are associated with low confidence scores caused by constraints on satisfying reasoning behavior patterns in demonstrations. We then adopt causal mediation analysis techniques to identify the attention heads responsible for these patterns. In addition, our findings indicate that LLMs retrieve factual and rule-based information for individual sub-reasoning tasks through specialized attention heads (approximately 3% total heads), whereas higher layers predominantly facilitate information integration and the emergence of global reasoning strategies (e.g., graph traversal algorithms) that coordinate multiple intermediate reasoning steps to solve the overall task.

sections.title: Start
sections.content: Phuong Minh Nguyen and Tien Huu Dang and Naoya Inoue Japan Advanced Institute of Science and Technology {phuongnm,tiendh,naoya-i}@jaist.ac.jp 6 2 0 2 7 2 ] . [ 1 4 2 8 7 2 . 5 0 6 2 : r a

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Recent studies have shown that Large Language Models (LLMs) can achieve strong reasoning performance by incorporating functional symbolic representations that abstractly describe graph traversal algorithms and stepby-step reasoning in few-shot learning settings. However, it remains unclear how LLMs genuinely understand the abstract meaning of each reasoning step and the overall algorithm from only limited number of demonstrations. This work aims to localize the attention heads responsible for individual reasoning steps and characterize the types of information transferred among them. We first align constituent reasoning steps with their corresponding token logits under symbolic-aided Chain-ofThought (CoT) prompting framework. Our analysis shows that token positions that steer the reasoning process are associated with low confidence scores caused by constraints on satisfying reasoning behavior patterns in demonstrations. We then adopt causal mediation analysis techniques to identify the attention heads responsible for these patterns. In addition, our findings indicate that LLMs retrieve factual and rule-based information for individual subreasoning tasks through specialized attention heads (approximately 3% total heads), whereas higher layers predominantly facilitate information integration and the emergence of global reasoning strategies (e.g., graph traversal algorithms) that coordinate multiple intermediate reasoning steps to solve the overall task.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Large Language Models (LLMs) and Chain-ofThought techniques (CoT) continue to demonstrate impressive performance across wide range of Natural Language Processing (NLP) tasks (Singh et al., 2025; Adcock et al., 2026; Dubey et al., 2024; Yang et al., 2025a; Brown et al., 2020; Wei et al., 2022). With the rapid advancement of LLMs, logical reasoning has become crucial research topic, 1 particularly in the context of explainable Artificial Intelligence (AI). Recent works show that LLMs still struggle with complex reasoning tasks (Huang and Chang, 2023; Yee et al., 2024; Ranaldi et al., 2025; Cheng et al., 2025). Numerous approaches have been proposed to improve the reasoning capabilities of LLMs, including prompt engineering (Xu et al., 2024b; Nguyen et al., 2025; Ranaldi et al., 2025), fine-tuning (Feng et al., 2024; Xu et al., 2024a), and the use of external symbolic solvers (Ye et al., 2023; Pan et al., 2023; Xu et al., 2024a). In contrast to previous works, this study does not focus on developing sophisticated stateof-the-art reasoning framework. Instead, it investigates fundamental research question: (RQ) What mechanisms do LLMs internally employ to solve logical reasoning tasks? Deductive reasoning in realistic settings remains challenging task, as it requires multi-hop reasoning to determine whether conclusion logically follows from given set of premises (Tafjord et al., 2021; Sun et al., 2024). In addition, recent studies (Nguyen et al., 2025; Ranaldi et al., 2025; Xu et al., 2024b) have shown that incorporating symbolic expressions into CoT prompting can improve the faithfulness of the LLM reasoning process. Therefore, we adopt the Symbolic-Aided CoT prompting format proposed by Nguyen et al. (2025) to effectively formalize the entire problem. In detail, we model the entire reasoning process as an inference graph constructed over predefined facts and rules (an example of deductive reasoning problem and its corresponding inference graph in Figure 1). Under this formulation, the reasoning problem can be viewed as graph traversal process, which can be employed to identify valid reasoning path from the initial facts to the target node representing the query. In this work, to address the aforementioned research question, we hypothesize that LLMs can learn, at an abstract level, the meaning of each inference step in the reasoning process through Figure 1: Overview of this framework. Given deductive reasoning problem description (xq) together with few-shot prompt containing demonstrations [(x1, y1), (x2, y2), xq] (left side of the figure), the LLM is expected to generate Chain-of-Thought reasoning process that follows the demonstrated template. The deductive reasoning problem can be represented as an inference graph composed of multiple inference steps, each of which can be further decomposed into salient reasoning components (e.g., premise selection) that are indicated by low-probability tokens across diverse LLMs (bottom graph in this figure). Subsequently, Causal Mediation Analysis techniques are employed to investigate the reasoning circuits of LLMs. few-shot demonstrations. Here, learn refers to the phenomenon in which LLMs activate specific subset of attention heads that serve as the primary mediators of the causal effect underlying the execution of inference steps and adherence to the designed graph-traversal algorithm. We adopt causal mediation analysis (CMA) (Pearl, 2001; Vig et al., 2020) techniques within the field of Mechanistic Interpretability to analyze the internal components responsible for the logical reasoning process and the interactions among them, commonly referred to as circuit (Olah et al., 2020; Meng et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2023). Diverse circuits in LLMs have been explored for specific tasks such as arithmetic reasoning (Stolfo et al., 2023), syllogistic reasoning (Kim et al., 2025), and propositional logical reasoning (Hong et al., 2026). However, previous works have primarily focused on simple inputoutput settings, lacking the complexity of real-world scenarios in which the output includes multi-step reasoning process preceding the final answer. Our work investigates the circuits responsible for LLMs executing reasoning strategy across multiple inference steps in CoT output. Overall, we first conduct preliminary experiments based on synthesized data to analyze the token positions that challenge LLMs in decoding the gold reasoning (referred to as uncertain tokens), and then apply CMA techniques to inspect which internal components affect logit changes at these uncertain tokens (bottom graph in Figure 1). We observe that high-confidence tokens are primarily associated with syntax or unambiguous reasoning actions. In contrast, uncertain tokens are the initial tokens that play crucial role in steering the reasoning process. Decoding these tokens is equivalent to satisfying multiple implicit constraints. For example, on the top-right side of Figure 1, consider the position of the premise selection token, (blue bounding rectangle). Three constraints must be satisfied: (1) the selected premise must correspond to fact that has already been proven true and appears in the KB snapshot variable; (2) the selected premise must satisfy at least one applicable rule (in this case, Rule3, Rule4, and Rule5 are valid); and (3) the selected premise must follow the traversal algorithm implied in the demonstrations (the breadth-first search (BFS) algorithm prefers selecting premise A, whereas the depth-first search (DFS) algorithm prefers selecting premise F). Similarly, the premise selection termination token (red bounding rectangle) determines whether the model should continue selecting additional premises for the current inference step. The rule selection token (yellow bounding rectangle) then determines which rule should be applied, given the selected premises. Interestingly, these uncertain positions remain 2 challenging across diverse LLM families and model sizes. Further details of the preliminary experimental setup and statistical results are provided in Section 2. Based on the preliminary experimental results, we categorize three major reasoning components within an inference step: premise selection, premise selection termination, rule selection. Next, we leverage CMA techniques to uncover the circuit network, collection of circuits where each circuit handles one reasoning component and exhibits strong interactions with other circuits in the network. We synthesize new deductive logical reasoning dataset containing pairs of clean and corrupted prompts for each reasoning component. By applying activation patching and path patching techniques (Meng et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2023), we extract the important subset of attention heads responsible for each reasoning component, as well as the graph of information flow transferred among them. We further validate the generalization of these circuits by knocking them out of LLMs and evaluating the modified models on well-known deductive logical reasoning benchmarks, including ProntoQA (Saparov and He, 2023), ProofWriter (Tafjord et al., 2021), and the general knowledge benchmark MMLU (Hendrycks et al., 2021). The performance on deductive logical reasoning tasks decreases dramatically, while performance on general knowledge tasks exhibits only slight decrease, thereby confirming the importance and generality of our discovered circuits for deductive logical reasoning.

sections.title: 2 Preliminary Experiment
sections.content: In this experiment, we aim to identify which tokens are important for steering the deductive logical reasoning behavior of LLMs within the CoT reasoning process. As illustrated by the token probability scores in Figure 1, these uncertain tokens are important because, when they are correctly predicted, the resulting logical reasoning process produces the correct final answer to the question. Dataset Construction. We adopt the Symbolicaided CoT prompting format (Nguyen et al., 2025) and construct synthesized dataset, where: (1) the premises are simplified and randomly selected from the uppercase alphabet characters, AZ, following prior work (Hong et al., 2026; Kim et al., 2025); (2) each sample contains demonstrations; (3) the total number of rules and facts is randomly generated between 8 and 18; and (4) the BFS algorithm is used to generate the reasoning chains. In addition, we filter out all ambiguous samples in which the question cannot be logically inferred from the given rules and facts. Ultimately, we obtain synthesized dataset, denoted as Dsyn shots with 500 samples for each {2, 3, 5, 7, 9}. Experimental Setup. We select 10% of Dsyn 9shots and feed all data points into four LLMs (Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, Qwen3-8B, Phi-4, and Qwen3-4B supported by transformers library), while caching the token probabilities from the last five shots. In addition, we categorize each token in the reasoning chain according to its role within each inference step of the overall reasoning process. Specifically, we define six token types (corresponding to six reasoning components), as shown in Table 1. Tokens with probabilities lower than 0.8 are identified as uncertain tokens and are subsequently used for analysis. Table 1: Reasoning component categories. The reasoning problem example is the same as Figure 1. Example Colored Reasoning Component KB = {A, K, F} => F(KB[A], Rule4) => => F(KB[F, K] Rule2) => syntax, premise in KB, premise selection, premise selection termination, rule selection, fact derivation Result Analysis and Motivation. We report the percentage of uncertain reasoning components and the distribution of their probabilities for Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, Qwen3-8B, Phi-4, and Qwen3-4B in Figures 7 and 6 in Appendix B. Our analysis reveals that three reasoning components account for the majority of the uncertainty, namely: = {premise selection, premise selection termination, rule selection}. As discussed in the introduction, these components play critical role in guiding the reasoning process. In contrast, most other tokens in the reasoning chain are assigned high probabilities (i.e., certain tokens). Therefore, if LLMs can correctly decode these uncertain tokens, they are likely to generate gold reasoning paths. This observation strongly motivates the investigation of the internal mechanisms or circuits responsible for these reasoning components. Although the synthesized dataset does not capture certain real-world complexities, such as variations in premise complexity, it preserves the intrinsic challenges of logical reasoning through multi3 hop reasoning and the number of rules that must be processed. In practice, on this synthesized dataset, Qwen3-8B and Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct achieve inference-step accuracies of approximately1 65% and 50%, respectively, highlighting the difficulty of the task. These findings further support the necessity of our research. We argue that the roles of attention heads in LLMs are shared across datasets that use the same task and format. To this end, we also conduct experiments on two well-known logical reasoning datasets, ProofWriter and ProntoQA, to further validate the generalization capability of our approach.

sections.title: 3 Methodology
sections.content: In the main target, we performed CMA techniques (Olah et al., 2020; Meng et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2023; Todd et al., 2024) to discover the important heads and circuits in transformer decoder-only LLMs (Vaswani et al., 2017) for each reasoning component (r) in R.

sections.title: 3.1 Background and Notation
sections.content: Given transformer-based LLM denoted by and k-shot prompt = [(x1, y1), . . . , (xk, yk), xq], the model sequentially predicts tokens to generate the output string yq = (p). At the output layer, yq is decoded as sequence of probability distributions over the vocabulary V, represented as [0, 1]yqV. For simplification in this paper, when analyzing specific reasoning token tr at known position pos(tr) in yq, we use the notation (p)[tr] = Ppos(tr)[tr] [0, 1] to denote the probability assigned to token tr at its position. For example, given at position 13 in the output for = premise selection, then (p)[A] represents the probability P13[A]. The residual stream of model at layer ℓ is computed as the sum of the previous layers hidden state, the projected multi-layer perceptron (MLP) output (mℓ), and the projected attention outputs aℓ,j, defined as: hℓ = hℓ1 + mℓ + (cid:80) aℓj where < and ℓ < L, with and denoting the number of layers and attention heads in , respectively. For the first layer (ℓ = 0), hℓ1 is initialized from the embedding layer. Prior work in mechanistic interpretability (MI) has established that attention layers primarily perform information routing and relational composition, while MLP layers function as key-value 1Detailed experimental results are provided in Section 4. memory stores for factual knowledge (Elhage et al., 2021; Meng et al., 2022), as further supported by the observations of Hong et al. (2026). Therefore, in this work, we focus only on the attention components, aℓj. 3.2 Circuit Discovery Given reasoning component R, we aim to discover the mechanism of model for decoding this component. Firstly, we adopt the activation patching technique (Vig et al., 2020; Todd et al., 2024) to score the contribution of each attention head. Then, we utilize the path patching (Wang et al., 2023) for computing the amount of information transferred between the pair of heads. Data preparation. Activation patching, which reveals circuit components by measuring how restoring clean activations recovers model performance on corrupted inputs, requires counterfactual pairs that differ precisely at the component of interest (Vig et al., 2020; Todd et al., 2024). Therefore, for each component, we construct synthesized dataset (Dpair) where clean (p) and corrupted prompts (p) share identical structure but differ in their causal context (Algorithm 1 in Appendix C). In these datasets, corrupted prompts systematically modify causal elements (e.g., fact values or rule definitions) to induce different token choice at the reasoning component position (e.g., premise selection), creating controlled comparison for activation patching. Table 2 shows the detailed comparison of our synthesized procedure (CORRUPT procedure in Algorithm 1). Activation Patching. Given the clean-corrupted prompting data pairs generated for corruption type (denoted by Dc for simplicity) from the previous step, the roles of LLM attention heads can be identified by measuring the average indirect effect (AIE) at the reasoning component of interest (tr). The AIE is computed by patching clean activations into the corrupted forward pass, quantifying each heads contribution to recovering the models clean-run behavior at position tr. AIEℓj = 1 Dc (cid:88) piDc (cid:0)f (pi aℓjs = aℓjs(pi))[tr ]f (p)[tr ](cid:1) (1) where aℓjs(pi) denotes the activation of head in layer ℓ at causal positions (s corrupted positions cached during the data synthesizing process) when the clean prompt is fed through model . Table 2: Procedure to synthesize the pair of clean and corrupted prompts. The corrupted position is the causal elements that affect the reasoning component of interest. Corrupted Position Component of Interest Procedure to Corrupt Prompt Target [Head roles] Facts (example in Table 3) premise selection (Corrupt type: = 1) Step 1. Randomly modify used fact. Step 2. Replace the corresponding premise change in the KB snapshot. Rules (example in Table 4) premise selection termination (c = 2) Step 1. Choose the last used rule in the reasoning chain if it has single premise condition, Rx Step 2. Replace the condition in Rx with an unseen premise. Step 3. Randomly choose an unused rule having double-premise condition, Ry Step 4. Replace the two conditional premises in Ry with: one conditional premise from Rx and one proven premise. Localize which heads read the facts by patching the causal span (corrupted positions), and which heads handle premise selection based on the facts by patching the previous (preceding) token of the component of interest. [Read Fact, Select Premise] Localize which heads read the rule condition by patching the causal span (both rules Rx,Ry), and which heads handle the matching of rule conditions with proven premises by patching the previous token of the component of interest. [Read Rule Condition, Match Rule Condition] Rules (example in Table 5) rule selection (c = 3) Step 1. Randomly choose one used rule in the reasoning chain, Rx Step 2. Replace the conditional premise of Rx with an unseen premise. Demonstrations (example in Table 6) premise selection (c = 4) Step 1. Use different traversal algorithm in demonstrations, ensuring that the corrupted reasoning chain is length equivalent to the clean reasoning chain for each demonstration. Localize which heads read the rule content by patching the causal span (content of Rx) and which heads handle rule selection based on premise matching by patching the preceding token. [Read Rule, Select Rule] Localize which heads read and implement the strategy of the traversal algorithm from few-shot demonstrations by patching demonstrations with the previous token of the component of interest. [Read Traversal Alg., Implement Traversal Alg.] Notably, we interpret the causal reading information heads (e.g., Read Fact) when the patching positions correspond to the causal spans (corrupted positions). In contrast, the reasoning component decision heads (e.g., Select Premise) are interpreted when the patching positions correspond to the preceding token of the focal reasoning component. We then select the top-k attention heads (Ac) with the highest causal indirect effect scores, which are considered responsible for corruption type within the reasoning component of interest. Path Patching. Path patching works by systematically corrupting and restoring activations between pairs of components to measure their causal influence on model outputs. Given the set of important attention heads = {Ac}, we aim to measure which heads communicate which information and quantify the strength of information flow. The combination of all subsets of heads responsible for each reasoning component reveals the interactions among reasoning circuits. For each pair of heads (heademit, headrec) = ((ℓ1, j1), (ℓ2, j2)) where (ℓ1 < ℓ2), following Wang et al. (2023), we first perform forward pass on the clean prompt while corrupting the activation at heademit, which in turn corrupts the causally dependent activation at headrec. We then patch only this corrupted activation of headrec into second forward pass of the clean prompt, allowing us to isolate and measure the causal dependence between this pair of heads. aℓ2j2s =aℓ2j2 (pi aℓ1j1s = aℓ1j1s(pi)) Si,(ℓ1j1)(ℓ2j2) =f (pi aℓ2j2s = aℓ2j2s)[tr 1 Dc S(ℓ1j1)(ℓ2j2) = (cid:0)Si,(ℓ1j1)(ℓ2j2) (cid:88) piDc (2) ] (pi)[tr ] (cid:1) (3) Finally, we obtained S(ℓ1j1)(ℓ2j2), ranking score that measures the average causal effect between pairs of attention heads in the reasoningresponsible subset of heads, A.

sections.title: 4 Experimental Results
sections.content: In this section, we present the experimental results and analyze the mechanism by which LLMs handle deductive logical reasoning tasks.

sections.title: 4.1 Localizing Attention Heads
sections.content: The distribution of AIE scores across layers and attention heads in the Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct model is shown in Figure 2 (the full version is provided in Figure 8). Results for the other models are presented in Figures 9,10, and 11. Additionally, the layer score for each reasoning decision head role (line chart) is computed by averaging the AIE scores of the top 15% highest-scoring heads within each layer. Reading Head. The results in Figure 8 reveal that causal information-reading heads (e.g., Read Fact) are concentrated in earlier layers compared to decision-making heads (e.g., Select Premise). 5 Figure 2: Distribution of AIE scores across layers and attention heads on Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct model. In the heatmap, the five highest-scoring heads are highlighted with green rectangles. In the line chart, the head role is aligned with the heat map; each layer score is computed by averaging the AIE scores of the top 15% highest-scoring heads in that layer; the red dashed line shows the layer most responsible for rule selection. This trend is consistent across all evaluated LLMs, demonstrating the generalizability of this observation. This distribution is intuitive: informationreading heads extract relevant facts from the problem description and propagate them through the residual stream to higher layers, where subsequent logical reasoning operations are performed. Decision Head. Comparing reasoning decision heads across LLMs (heatmaps in Figures 2,9,10, and 11), we observe that heads responsible for matching rule conditions are concentrated in the middle layers and correspond to the earliest reasoning stage in the computational pipeline. This observation is intuitively consistent with human reasoning, where rule conditions must first be validated before determining which rules should be applied. In addition, heads responsible for rule selection exhibit high sparsity, with small number of heads accounting for dominant proportion of the effect. Notably, the highest AIE score in Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct exceeds 30%, suggesting that the model relies heavily on single head for rule selection decisions. similar pattern is observed in the Qwen models, where the peak AIE scores exceed 12%. We hypothesize that this sparsity arises because rule selection occurs after premise selection in the reasoning pipeline of prompting design (Figure 1), making it more deterministic operation that can be handled by small number of specialized, high-impact heads. On the other hand, the line graphs on Figure 2 reveal consistent temporal computational structure across all LLMs: matching rule condition implementing traversal algorithm selecting premise and rule premise decision (termination). Figure 3: Circuit network on top-5 attention head scores associated with reasoning components.

sections.title: 4.2 Circuit Network
sections.content: Here, we analyze collection of sub-circuits corresponding to reasoning components, referred to as circuit network. For each reasoning component, we analyze the top-5 heads for each reasoning role and the top-10 strongest causal effects between pairs of heads computed by path patching score (S) (Figure 3, 12, 13, 14). Circuit Specialization. The results show that for each circuit, each type of reasoning head typically transfers information corresponding to its role. For example, in the Rule Condition Matching circuit, information about the rule condition is trans6 ferred from the reading heads to the decision heads (e.g., rule condition information is transferred from (L11H12 (stand for layer 11 head 12), L12H3) to L19H12, (L10H2, L11H12, L12H12) to L11H4). In addition, the information for each sub-reasoning task is transferred and integrated into the decision heads at the deeper layer (L19 for Rule Condition Matching, L17 and L28 for Rule Selection, L18 and L30 for Premise Selection). This clearly confirms the characteristic of temporal computational structure observed in the previous section. Circuit Interaction. Overall, many attention heads perform multiple reasoning sub-tasks (polysemantic attention head) and the polysemantic decision heads are the center of integration of information. For instance, L10H1, L10H2, and L11H12 perform three types of causal information reading: read rule condition, read rule, and read fact; polysemantic decision head, L17H24, handles multiple reasoning decision sub-tasks. Different LLMs exhibit distinct polysemantic patterns: Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct shares heads primarily for causal reading roles, whereas Qwen models share heads across decision-making roles.

sections.title: 4.3 Ablation LR Heads Across Datasets
sections.content: To further confirm the necessity of deductive LR heads, we evaluate their impact across our synthesized dataset Dsyn, presented in Section 2, established LR benchmarks (ProntoQA (Saparov and He, 2023), ProofWriter (Tafjord et al., 2021)), and general knowledge dataset (MMLU (Hendrycks et al., 2021)). In both LR benchmarks, we apply Symbolic-Aided CoT prompting following the setting from previous works (Nguyen et al., 2025, 2026). We ablate (knock out) the top-k important heads by setting aℓj = 0 when computing hℓj (Section 3.1) for all tokens associated with the ablated heads (both the prefill and decoding stage), for each reasoning component role identified by the AIE scores. We then measure the resulting performance degradation and compare it against random head ablation across LLMs. Experimental Setup. Since models differ in total head count, we set = 8 for phi-4 and = 5 for Qwen and Llama models. We evaluate six configurations: (baseline) evaluates original model performance; /Rand ablates 3% of heads uniformly at random and average over three random independent run; /RS ablates top-k reading rule and top-k rule selection heads ( 1% of total heads); /PS ablates top-k reading fact and top-k premise selection heads ( 1%); /PST ablates top-k reading rule condition and top-k matching rule condition heads ( 1%); /3Roles ablates all heads from the previous three configurations (/RS, /PS, and /PST, totaling 3% of heads). For the evaluation, on MMLU, PronQA, and ProofWriter, we primarily used final-answer accuracy. For the synthesized data, we used inferencestep accuracy, which measures the proportion of correct reasoning steps (or correct inference steps over the rule space) rather than considering only the final answer, which helps more accurately evaluate the reasoning ability of LLMs. Figure 4: Inference step accuracy after knocking out top-k logical reasoning heads on synthesized data. Figure 5: Effect of knocking out top-k logical reasoning heads on MMLU and ProntoQA datasets. Results and Analysis. The results of this experiment are shown in Figures 4, 5 and in Figure 15, Appendix E. On the synthesized data, ablating the LR heads significantly decreases performance across few-shot configurations compared to ablating uniformly random heads, even when the number of ablated heads is three times smaller in the /RS, /PS, and /PST settings. In particular, ablating the three important reasoning components (setting 7 /3Roles) causes the reasoning ability of all LLMs to collapse to nearly zero. 2025). Unlike these works, our work investigates deductive reasoning circuits in LLMs. On two well-known LR benchmarks, ProofWriter and ProntoQA, the results show the same trend across LLMs, demonstrating that the LR heads identified by our method on synthesized data generalize well and are not biased toward domainor data-specific features. In addition, we observe that the accuracies of 44.6% on ProntoQA and 29.2% on ProofWriter for phi-4 (similar behavior observed for other models) are mainly due to random guessing of the final answer after generating an incorrect reasoning chain. This is because ProntoQA contains two possible answers (True/False), whereas ProofWriter contains three possible answers (True/False/Uncertain). In other words, the LLMs only preserve the reasoning format than performing meaningful reasoning process. On the MMLU dataset, ablating individual reasoning component types (/RS, /PS, /PST) yields performance drops comparable to random ablation. However, the /3Roles setting, which ablates all LR heads simultaneously, causes substantially larger degradation than random ablation of the same proportion. This suggests that while individual LR components have limited impact on general knowledge tasks, their collective contribution is significant, indicating that deductive reasoning heads are recruited even for broad knowledge retrieval and application. rather

sections.title: 5 Related Work
sections.content: Logical Reasoning in LLMs. Recent studies reveal that LLMs struggle with complex reasoning tasks (Yee et al., 2024; Ranaldi et al., 2025; Cheng et al., 2025). To address these limitations, researchers have explored multiple approaches, including: prompt engineering (Xu et al., 2024b; Nguyen et al., 2025; Ranaldi et al., 2025), finetuning (Feng et al., 2024; Xu et al., 2024a), incorporating symbolic expressions into Chain-ofThought prompting (Nguyen et al., 2025; Ranaldi et al., 2025; Xu et al., 2024b), leveraging external symbolic solvers where LLMs translate natural language into formal logic (Pan et al., 2023; Ye et al., 2023; Olausson et al., 2023; Gao et al., 2023; Yao et al., 2023; Xu et al., 2024a), and developing interactive prompting frameworks that decompose complex reasoning into manageable subtasks (Zhou et al., 2023; Zhang et al., 2025; Kazemi et al., 2023; Feng et al., 2024; Sun et al., 2024; Lee and Hwang, 8 Reasoning Circuits in LLMs. In recent works, various circuits have been discovered for specialized tasks, including factual knowledge recall (Meng et al., 2022), indirect object identification (Stolfo et al., 2023), time-specific information (Park et al., 2025), arithmetic reasoning (Stolfo et al., 2023), abstract reasoning with emergent symbolic (Yang et al., 2025b), syllogistic reasoning (Kim et al., 2025), and propositional logical reasoning (Hong et al., 2026). However, prior circuit analysis work has focused on simple inputoutput mappings rather than multi-step reasoning processes. Our work addresses this gap by decomposing complex deductive reasoning into constituent reasoning components and identifying the circuits responsible for each step.

sections.title: 6 Conclusion
sections.content: In this work, we investigated the mechanisms underlying deductive reasoning in LLMs by localizing and characterizing the attention heads responsible for multi-step logical inference. Using causal mediation analysis, we identified specialized attention heads associated with different reasoning components. Our analysis reveals hierarchical structure in which early-to-middle layers retrieve and process factual and rule-based information, while higher layers integrate this information and coordinate global reasoning strategies. We further found that reasoning-critical token positions consistently exhibit low confidence scores, reflecting the strict reasoning constraints induced by few-shot demonstrations. By ablating the identified reasoning heads across multiple datasets, including our synthesized benchmark, ProntoQA, ProofWriter, and MMLU, we showed that these circuits are essential for deductive reasoning, with their removal causing substantially larger performance degradation than random ablation. Overall, our findings provide mechanistic insights into how LLMs internalize abstract reasoning algorithms from limited demonstrations, revealing sparse and modular circuit architecture that supports complex multi-hop inference. This work may contribute to improving reasoning robustness, interpretability, and targeted interventions for future language models.

sections.title: Limitations
sections.content: Our analysis is primarily conducted on synthesized datasets with explicit logical structures, which may not fully generalize to free-form text reasoning or implicit chain-of-thought processes in naturalistic contexts. Additionally, we focus on attention mechanisms while other components (e.g., MLP layers) may also contribute to reasoning. The identified circuits are specific to the models and datasets studied; their generalization across different architectures, scales, and reasoning domains requires further investigation.

sections.title: A AI Usage Declaration
sections.content: AI tools were used for grammar checking and formatting of tables and figures, and polish writing. All technical content and implementations were written by the authors.

sections.title: B Preliminary experiments
sections.content: We present the percentage of uncertain reasoning components and the distribution of their probabilities for the Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, Qwen3-8B, phi-4, and Qwen3-4B models in Figure 6. The experimental results show the same trend across LLMs. 11 Figure 6: Distribution of uncertain token (low token probability) grouped by reasoning component on Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct and Qwen3-8B. Figure 7: Distribution of uncertain token (low token probability) grouped by reasoning component on phi-4 and Qwen3-4B.

sections.title: C Data Synthesizing Algorithm
sections.content: To identify reasoning circuit components, we construct synthesized dataset Dpair of structurally identical prompt pairs: clean prompts and corrupted prompts (Algorithm 1). Each corrupted prompt systematically modifies causal elements (e.g., fact values, rule definitions) that influence token selection at specific reasoning positions, such as premise selection or rule application. This controlled perturbation enables activation patching experiments that isolate component-level causal contributions to reasoning behavior. Algorithm 1 Corrupted Prompt Generation Require: data points, demonstrations, corrupt type Ensure: Dataset Dpair(c) = {(pi, pi)}n 1: while < 10 and do 2: 3: 4: 5: 6: // Generate k-shots (problem and reasoning chain) (xz, yz) GENERATELRPROBLEM() for = 0 to + 1 do i=1 end for pi FORMAT({(xz, yz)}k+1 z=0) // Corrupt clean prompt pi CORRUPT(pi, c) 7: 8: // Validate pi and pi share identical structure if VALIDATESTRUCTURE(pi, pi) then Dpair(c) Dpair(c) {(pi, pi)} 9: 10: 11: 12: 13: 14: end while 15: return Dpair(c) end if + 1 Table 3: Example of corrupt prompt type: modifying the facts affects premise selection. The blue text represents the content of the causal span (as well as the corrupted positions) in the clean prompt, while the red text represents the corrupted content, and the gray text will be ignored. The last corrupted position corresponds to the reasoning component, where we inspect the logit difference under changes in the causal spans (causal positions). ### Given list of facts and rules: # (Rule1): is true # (Rule2): is true # (Rule3): is true is true # (Rule4): is true # (Rule5): is true # (Rule6): is true # (Rule7): If D, then # (Rule8): If then # (Rule9): If J, then # (Rule10): If then # (Rule11): If P, then # (Rule12): If K, then # (Rule13): If then # (Rule14): If then # (Rule15): If then # (Rule16): If then # (Rule17): If then # (Rule18): If O, then # (Rule19): If then # (Rule20): If M, then # (Rule21): If then # (Question): truth value of P? # (Answer): Start from the object mentioned in the question: KB = {V, S, K, E, L, A} => F(KB[Q ], Rule10) => KB = {V, S, Q, E, L, A, P} => F(KB[V], Rule15) => KB = {V, S, Q, E, L, A, P, F} => F(KB[F], Rule19) => KB = {V, S, Q, E, L, A, P, F, U} => Validate(KB, Question=P) = True.

sections.title: D Circuit Discovering Results
sections.content: This section contains additional circuit discovery results across LLMs (Figures 8, 9, 10, 11, and 12, 13, 14), which could not be fully presented in the main paper due to space limitations.

sections.title: E Ablating LR Heads Across Datasets
sections.content: This section presents additional experiments on knocking out logical reasoning (LR) heads in LLMs to further validate the importance of deductive reasoning heads not only on the synthesized dataset but also on well-known logical reasoning dataset, ProofWriter.

sections.title: F Synthesize Data Example
sections.content: For better understanding of the synthesis process, this section presents examples of clean-corrupt prompt pairs in Dpair(c) across different corruption types (c), as shown in Tables 3,4,5, and 6. Table 4: Example of corrupt prompt type: modifying rule content affects premise selection termination. ### Given list of facts and rules: # (Rule1): If R, then # (Rule1): If V, then # (Rule2): If Q, then # (Rule3): If then # (Rule4): If then # (Rule5): If then # (Rule6): If then # (Rule7): If then # (Rule8): If then # (Rule8): If then # (Rule9): If then # (Rule10): If U, then # (Rule11): If then # (Rule12): If then # (Rule13): If then # (Rule14): If N, then # (Rule15): If N, then # (Rule16): If V, then # (Rule17): is true # (Rule18): is true # (Rule19): is true # (Rule20): is true # (Rule21): is true # (Rule22): is true # (Question): truth value of N? # (Answer): Start from the object mentioned in the question: KB = {L, O, H, T, U, B} => F(KB[U], Rule6) => KB = {L, O, H, T, U, B, C} => F(KB[U, C], Rule10) => KB = {L, O, H, T, U, B, C, V} => F(KB[V], , Rule8) => KB = {L, O, H, T, U, B, C, V, N} => Validate(KB, Question=N) = True. 12 Figure 8: Distribution of AIE scores across layers and attention heads on Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct model. In the heatmap, the five highest-scoring heads are highlighted with green rectangles. The head roles in the line chart are aligned with the second row heat map; each layer score is computed by averaging the AIE scores of the top 15% highest-scoring heads in that layer; the red dashed line show the layer most responsible for rule selection. Figure 9: Distribution of AIE scores across layers and attention heads on Qwen3-8B model. 13 Figure 10: Distribution of AIE scores across layers and attention heads on Qwen3-4B model. Figure 11: Distribution of AIE scores across layers and attention heads on phi-4 model.. 14 Figure 12: Circuit network on top-5 attention head scores associated with reasoning components in the Qwen3-8B model. Figure 13: Circuit network on top-5 attention head scores associated with reasoning components in the Qwen3-4B model. 15 Figure 14: Circuit network on top-5 attention head scores associated with reasoning components in the phi-4 model. Figure 15: Inference step accuracy when knockout top-k logical reasoning heads on synthesized data (Dsyn) and on Logical Reasoning task, ProofWriter dataset (k = 8 with phi-4 model and = 5 with other models). 16 Table 5: Example of corrupt prompt type: modifying rule content affects the rule selection component. ### Given list of facts and rules: # (Rule1): If J, then # (Rule2): If then # (Rule2): If then # (Rule3): If C, then # (Rule4): If then # (Rule5): If then # (Rule6): If O, then # (Rule7): If S, then # (Rule8): If C, then # (Rule9): If then # (Rule10): If then # (Rule11): If U, then # (Rule12): If Q, then # (Rule13): If R, then # (Rule14): is true # (Rule15): is true # (Rule16): is true # (Rule17): is true # (Question): truth value of N? # (Answer): Start from the object mentioned in the question: KB = { O, M, J, F} => F(KB[O M], Rule2 5 ) => KB = {O, M, J, F, U} => F(KB[M], Rule5) => KB = {O, M, J, F, U, N} => Validate(KB, Question=N) = True. Table 6: Example of corrupt prompt type: changing the traversal algorithm in demonstrations affects the premise selection component. ### Given list of facts and rules: # (Rule1): If then # (Rule2): If O, then # (Rule3): If then # (Rule4): If then # (Rule5): If S, then # (Rule6): If L, then # (Rule7): If then # (Rule8): If J, then # (Rule9): is true # (Rule10): is true # (Rule11): is true # (Question): truth value of F? # (Answer): Start from the object mentioned in the question: KB = {S, N, P} => F(KB[N], Rule4) => KB = {S, N, P, L} => F(KB[L P], Rule1 7) => I KB = {S, N, P, L, I} => F(KB[P L], Rule7 1) => J KB = {S, N, P, L, I, J} => F(KB[L, I], Rule6) => KB = {S, N, P, L, I, J, F} => Validate(KB, Question=F) = True. - ### Given list of facts and rules: # (Rule1): If then # (Rule2): If then # (Rule3): If then # (Rule4): If then # (Rule5): If then # (Rule6): If then # (Rule7): If then # (Rule8): If A, then # (Rule9): is true # (Rule10): is true # (Rule11): is true # (Question): truth value of V? # (Answer): Start from the object mentioned in the question: KB = {L, Q, I} => F(KB[L], Rule2) => KB = {L, Q, I, B} => F(KB[B Q], Rule6 7) => KB = {L, Q, I, B, V} => Validate(KB, Question=V) = True. - ### Given list of facts and rules: # (Rule1): If then # (Rule2): If then # (Rule3): If then # (Rule4): If A, then # (Rule5): If then # (Rule6): If then # (Rule7): If then # (Rule8): If then # (Rule9): If O, then # (Rule10): If then # (Rule11): If then # (Rule12): If W, then # (Rule13): If V, then # (Rule14): If V, then # (Rule15): If F, then # (Rule16): If Q, then # (Rule17): is true # (Rule18): is true # (Rule19): is true # (Rule20): is true # (Rule21): is true # (Rule22): is true # (Question): truth value of V? # (Answer): Start from the object mentioned in the question: KB = {J, M, R, I, A, B} => F(KB[R], Rule2) => KB = {J, M, R, I, A, B, O} => F(KB[O ], Rule11) => KB = {J, M, R, I, A, B, O, V} => Validate(KB, Question=V) = True.
